paragraphs.body: I remember waking up every Sunday morning and I would walk out of my room to go right into the kitchen to see what my mom was up to. She would tell me to eat something then go get ready because we were all going to the park to play on the playground and ride our bikes. I would hurry up to eat my food, letting my brother know what was going on so he could hurry up as well. Then, I ran back to my room and put my blue shorts on with a softball shirt. I was very happy though because I knew that when we got to the park we would “accidentally” run into my aunt with her dog Halo (who I absolutely love). After running into her that would always mean we would get lunch with her when we were all down with the park. While we were at the park, we would all just ride our bikes around, through the trails, up and down all the hills the entire morning to late afternoon. But this one Sunday morning my mom said no to going to the park.

“Life isn’t fair” is what she kept saying. This phrase is what I often heard growing up. My mother would always use this phrase, especially when I really needed to hear it. I heard it a lot that Sunday morning and I wasn’t happy about it because I truly never understood what she meant when she would say it. I asked multiple times that morning about why we weren’t going to the park and all she kept saying back was “life isn’t fair.” When others would hear her say it, they would think she was being mean and insulting us, little did they know she was teaching us a lesson. You may wonder what lesson...we call it the lesson of life. There was another time when I was about 13 years old and there was this one time when I was with my friends, and I had to leave early due to softball practice and the whole way there I was just complaining. I was so upset with my parents because I didn’t understand why I had to leave my friend's house to go to practice. I just didn’t know why I couldn’t have just skipped practice or just be late to practice to hang out with my friends a little bit longer. Then, my mom hit me with “life isn’t fair.” Ever since then I always looked deeper into the meaning of it because she is right. Life isn’t fair, it is not fair in many unusual ways. Without my mom saying that phrase to me back then I would not be where I am today. My mother taught me a lesson that day. That phrase to me is particularly important. It means that life isn’t easy. There will be many challenging times, and you have to get through those to get to the better times.

However, this isn’t what you would normally hear in my household. For people who aren’t in my family they wouldn’t know about how we act or the phrases we use. No, instead people think the absolute worst when they hear something out of pocket or a little criticism. Some people have the idea that my mother can be very horrible to us kids because they don’t understand our language. My ‘home’ language is the language I speak with my family which no one truly understands besides us. I believe these few catch phrases make us closer than I am with others. Another example of being thinking my mom is harsh on us is when my mom and I were out with my little sister, Kendall, to go shopping. Then my little sister kept asking my mom for things and she kept on saying no to her. There was this one thing that she wanted; it was this sweatshirt. She wanted it so badly that she was holding it the whole time we were in the store. Right when we were going to check out Kendall asked her for the 50th time if she could get this sweatshirt and you know what my mom says. She says, “no, hit the bricks.” That phrase is a very popular one in my family, especially for my mom. When people have heard that they think of her being rude and not caring for us.

“Hey mom. Do you think I can get this sweatshirt?” “No, honey.” “Hey momma, do you think I can buy these glasses on Amazon?” “No, Kenz.” “Hey mom, do you think I can buy these new shoes?” “Maybe honey, but no promises.” “Hey momma, do you think I can get these leggings? They are on sale.” “No, sweetie. Stop asking me for things. “Hey momma, do you think I can get this pajama set?”

“No, Maria. Go hit the bricks.” In the end, despite all of that has happened throughout the years and all the phrases my mom has said to us kids in public are the prime example of never judging a book by a cover. I don’t like wearing sweatshirts in 45-degree weather; I’m not sitting here waiting for someone to teach me a life lesson; I don’t wear fancy preppy clothes; I don’t like making videos of me dancing or getting ready for my day. I’m just a normal Pitt student who also plays Pitt softball. I do appreciate a clean look, but I don’t like it when you’re trying too hard. I like talking about my family even if it means bringing up old memories; I like taking pictures and not posting them; I like buying things, but I always ask before; I love my family even if it means people think they treat me wrongly. At the end of the day, I am proud to be involved with my family. I love them because they have made me who I am today. The phrases that have brought me up I am thankful for. Because without the phrases I wouldn’t have understood how important life is and why it’s okay to say no. Me saying these phrases will help me prove others wrong when they thought the worse when they heard my mom say them.

annotations.comment: I don't see any revision comments here, but I compared your previous draft with this one and see you've made some minor line edits (which improve the flow and style of your writing!), but no big changes to content, structure, or form. But good work!
